problem: Determine the values of \( p \in \mathbb{R} \) for which the integral \( \int_1^\infty x^p \cos(\ln x) \, dx \) converges absolutely and conditionally.

steps.text:
To determine the convergence of the integral \(\int_1^\infty x^p \cos(\ln x) \, dx\), we first consider the absolute convergence by examining \(\int_1^\infty |x^p \cos(\ln x)| \, dx = \int_1^\infty x^p |\cos(\ln x)| \, dx\). Since \(|\cos(\ln x)| \leq 1\), we have \(x^p |\cos(\ln x)| \leq x^p\). The integral \(\int_1^\infty x^p \, dx\) converges if and only if \(p < -1\). Therefore, the integral \(\int_1^\infty x^p |\cos(\ln x)| \, dx\) converges if \(p < -1\). This means the integral converges absolutely for \(p < -1\).

For conditional convergence, we need to check if the integral \(\int_1^\infty x^p \cos(\ln x) \, dx\) converges when \(p \geq -1\). We will use integration by parts. Let \(u = \cos(\ln x)\) and \(dv = x^p \, dx\). Then \(du = -\frac{\sin(\ln x)}{x} \, dx\) and \(v = \frac{x^{p+1}}{p+1}\). Applying integration by parts, we get:

\[
\int_1^\infty x^p \cos(\ln x) \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^{p+1} \cos(\ln x)}{p+1} \right]_1^\infty - \int_1^\infty \frac{x^{p+1}}{p+1} \left( -\frac{\sin(\ln x)}{x} \right) \, dx
\]

Simplifying the right-hand side, we have:

\[
\int_1^\infty x^p \cos(\ln x) \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^{p+1} \cos(\ln x)}{p+1} \right]_1^\infty + \frac{1}{p+1} \int_1^\infty x^p \sin(\ln x) \, dx
\]

The term \(\left[ \frac{x^{p+1} \cos(\ln x)}{p+1} \right]_1^\infty\) converges if and only if \(p > -2\). For \(p = -2\), the term is \(\left[ \frac{x^{-1} \cos(\ln x)}{-1} \right]_1^\infty = \left[ -\frac{\cos(\ln x)}{x} \right]_1^\infty\), which converges because \(\cos(\ln x)\) is bounded and \(\frac{1}{x} \to 0\) as \(x \to \infty\). For \(p < -2\), the term diverges. Therefore, the integral converges conditionally for \(-2 \leq p < -1\).

Thus, the integral converges absolutely for \(p < -1\) and conditionally for \(-2 \leq p < -1\).

\[
\boxed{p < -1}
\]

problem: Determine the limit \( \lim\limits_{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sin x}{|x|^\alpha} \) for \( \alpha > 0 \), if it exists, using the definition of a limit at infinity.
